title: Pack-Density And Clean-Air Rejoin Proxies
summary: F1 strategy analysis emphasizes that undercut/overcut depends on being in range of rivals and on whether a stop drops the car into traffic.
rationale: F1 strategy analysis emphasizes that undercut/overcut depends on being in range of rivals and on whether a stop drops the car into traffic. The current best features use medians, but not explicit pack-density or local rejoin-pressure proxies.
implementation_hint: Within each Race_Year x LapNumber, create ordered-field features: position percentile, local neighborhood density, lap-time and delta gaps to adjacent position ranks, field dispersion, and whether the driver sits near a probable rejoin pocket based on estimated pit-loss classes from track-level priors. Add interactions with LapTime_Delta, Position_Change, and compound warmup regime.
expected_effect: Should capture pit timing driven by traffic and strategic windows rather than tyre life alone, especially on tracks where track position is decisive.
risk: The dataset lacks true time gaps, so these are proxies; if approximated too crudely they may add noise or duplicate existing group-median features.
